items.q: Does groundwater refill as quickly as it is pumped out?
items.correct: Usually not — aquifer recharge can take decades to millennia, so abstraction above the recharge rate effectively mines the store and water tables fall.
items.misconception: Groundwater is effectively unlimited because rain constantly tops it up.
items.random_wrong: Groundwater is manufactured in water treatment plants.
items.why: Students assume the cycle rebalances instantly; slow aquifer recharge is why over-abstraction causes long-term insecurity.

items.q: Is water scarcity only caused by dry climates?
items.correct: No — economic water scarcity occurs where rainfall is adequate but infrastructure and investment are lacking, as in much of sub-Saharan Africa.
items.misconception: Only desert countries can suffer water insecurity.
items.random_wrong: Water scarcity only happens in winter.
items.why: The physical versus economic scarcity distinction is central to this topic and frequently missed.

items.q: Do large dams solve water insecurity?
items.correct: Only partially — reservoirs store and regulate supply, but they lose water to evaporation, trap sediment, displace communities and reduce flows downstream, sometimes shifting insecurity elsewhere.
items.misconception: Building a big dam permanently fixes a region's water problems.
items.random_wrong: Dams work by creating new rainfall.
items.why: Students treat hard engineering as a complete fix, ignoring evaporation losses, costs and downstream impacts.

items.q: Lag time on a storm hydrograph is the time between:
items.misconception: The start and end of the storm
items.why: Correct answer: Peak rainfall and peak discharge. “The start and end of the storm” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Physical water scarcity differs from economic water scarcity because:
items.correct: Supply itself is insufficient, rather than access and infrastructure being lacking
items.misconception: It only occurs in wealthy countries
items.random_wrong: It is caused by water being too expensive
items.why: Correct answer: Supply itself is insufficient, rather than access and infrastructure being lacking. “It only occurs in wealthy countries” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which process moves water from vegetation into the atmosphere?
items.why: It's Transpiration. “Infiltration” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Flashier — shorter lag time and higher peak — because impermeable surfaces speed up runoff
items.misconception: Flatter, with a longer lag time and lower peak
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Flashier — shorter lag time and higher peak — because impermeable surfaces speed up runoff. “Flatter, with a longer lag time and lower peak” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A drainage basin receives 1,200 mm of precipitation a year and loses 700 mm to evapotranspiration. With no change in storage, what is the annual runoff in mm?
items.why: The real figure is 500 mm. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
